food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — grilled skewer

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Satay Babat. Closest match is beef tripe food product (FOODON_03315671); the satay preparation and marinade are not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers prepared beef offal/tripe. The specific satay-style charcoal-grilled preparation with Malay spice marinade is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Satay Babat or beef tripe satay as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 200 g, including tripe skewers, peanut sauce, and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Satay Babat consists of 4–6 skewers of grilled beef tripe (approximately 120–150 g) accompanied by peanut sauce, compressed rice (ketupat), cucumber slices, and raw onion, totalling approximately 200 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, variety meats and by-products, tripe, cooked (NDB No. 13322); supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for Malay satay preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients are adapted from USDA FoodData Central beef tripe (cooked) values, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD satay preparation data. Marinade ingredients (palm sugar, spices, peanut sauce) contribute to carbohydrate, sugar, and sodium values. Values represent the composite dish including marinade and sauce as typically consumed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on high-protein, moderate-fat grilled meat skewer with spiced marinade
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Satay Babat. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on grilled meat skewer with spiced marinade entries in the International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., 2021). The high protein (12.5 g/100 g) and moderate fat (10.2 g/100 g) content of beef tripe suppress postprandial glucose response. Palm sugar in the marinade contributes modest carbohydrates (5.8 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 5.8 × 2) / 100 = 4.6 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g) from marinade and peanut sauce; cholesterol from beef tripe (95 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Satay Babat to small portions and request reduced peanut sauce, as the combined sodium from marinade and sauce can contribute substantially to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; cholesterol intake from tripe should also be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low carbohydrate content (5.8 g/100 g); high protein supports satiety and glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Satay Babat has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large portions of ketupat (compressed rice) and with peanut sauce used sparingly to limit added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from beef tripe (95 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (3.1 g/100 g); partially offset by anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Satay Babat in moderation due to its cholesterol and saturated fat content from beef tripe; the anti-inflammatory marinade spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal) may offer modest cardioprotective benefit, but sodium from peanut sauce remains a concern for blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds in turmeric (curcumin) and ginger in the marinade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and ginger in the Satay Babat marinade contain anti-inflammatory compounds that may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though some patients may find organ meats or charcoal-grilled foods to be personal dietary triggers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay Babat is a traditional Malay grilled skewer dish made from beef tripe (babat) that has been thoroughly cleaned, boiled until tender, marinated in a complex spice blend, and then charcoal-grilled on skewers. The marinade typically includes lemongrass, turmeric, ginger, galangal, coriander seeds, and palm sugar, imparting a deeply aromatic, slightly sweet, and earthy flavour profile. The tripe has a uniquely chewy, dense texture that absorbs the smoky charcoal aroma and the layered spice marinade exceptionally well. It is traditionally served with spicy peanut sauce (kuah kacang), compressed rice (ketupat), fresh cucumber slices, and raw onion wedges. Satay Babat is popular as a street food snack, appetiser, or main dish at Malay food stalls and hawker centres in Malaysia and Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie, high-protein dish (12.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (10.2 g/100 g) and low carbohydrates (5.8 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The sodium content (380 mg/100 g) is elevated due to the marinade and peanut sauce, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The cholesterol content from beef tripe (~95 mg/100 g) warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular risk. The anti-inflammatory spices in the marinade — particularly turmeric and ginger — may offer modest health benefits. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Satay Babat?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are adapted from USDA FoodData Central beef tripe (cooked) values cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD satay preparation data. No single approved source provides an exact nutritional entry for Satay Babat as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite nature of the dish and the absence of a direct exact-match entry in any single approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the composite dish (tripe skewers with marinade and peanut sauce) as typically consumed; values adapted from USDA FoodData Central beef tripe (cooked, NDB 13322) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD satay preparation data. 2. Saturated fat (3.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA beef tripe fatty acid profile; these values carry moderate uncertainty for the composite dish. 3. Cholesterol (95 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA beef tripe cooked data. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite Satay Babat dish (tripe + marinade + peanut sauce) is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central (NDB 13322) or IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this specific composite preparation; the multi-component nature of the dish (tripe, spice marinade, peanut sauce, accompaniments) makes a reliable single moisture value unavailable from any approved source as of April 2026. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on grilled meat skewer with spiced marinade from Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI Tables; no direct GI measurement for Satay Babat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 5.8 × 2) / 100 = 4.6 g (low GL). 6. Peanut allergy: peanut sauce (kuah kacang) is a primary allergen concern. 7. The dish is popular in Malaysia and Singapore; country of origin is Malaysia with significant presence in Singapore hawker culture. 8. No zero-value core macros are present; all core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are non-zero and supported.
